trade.summary: The United States is Switzerland's largest single-country trading partner. Switzerland exports a large surplus driven by pharmaceuticals, chemicals, watches, and financial services. The US exports aircraft, machinery, and IT products. Bilateral trade is underpinned by shared economic values and deep investment ties.
disputes: US pressure on Swiss banking secrecy (largely resolved via FATCA agreement)

diplomatic.summary: Strong bilateral relations built on shared democratic values and deep economic ties. Switzerland represents US interests in countries where Washington lacks diplomatic missions (Cuba, Iran historically). The US is Switzerland's most important partner outside Europe. Swiss neutrality occasionally creates divergence on sanctions policy.

military.summary: Switzerland is neutral and not a NATO member, but shares security values with the US. US military has historically used Swiss airspace. Swiss defense purchases include US equipment. Intelligence sharing occurs through informal channels. No joint exercises.

financial.summary: Massive bilateral financial ties. US is the largest source of FDI into Switzerland and vice versa. Swiss banks have major US operations; US banks and investment firms operate in Zurich and Geneva. FATCA framework governs tax information exchange. Bilateral investment stock exceeds USD 300 billion.

energy.summary: Limited direct energy trade. Switzerland imports some US LNG through European networks. US energy companies (ExxonMobil, Chevron) have Swiss offices. Cooperation on clean energy innovation.

scientific.summary: Among the strongest bilateral scientific partnerships globally. ETH Zurich and US research universities (MIT, Stanford, Caltech) maintain deep programs. Major pharmaceutical R&D collaboration. Joint Nobel laureates. Switzerland is among the top recipients of US-Swiss joint research grants.
